exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.title: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.

items.sentence: Historians have yet to find a satisfactory (1) ...... (EXPLAIN) for how powered flight developed so rapidly, but most agree that the (2) ...... (INTRODUCE) of the internal combustion engine was the key turning point.
items.explanation: Noun from EXPLAIN → satisfactory **explanation**; noun from INTRODUCE → **introduction** of the engine.

items.sentence: Many (3) ...... (SCIENCE) had long dismissed the idea of human flight as fantasy, insisting there was no (4) ...... (POSSIBLE) that anyone would ever master the art of (5) ...... (BUILD) a machine capable of carrying passengers through the air.
items.explanation: People who study science → **scientists**; noun from POSSIBLE → no **possibility**; gerund from BUILD → art of **building** a machine.

items.sentence: Nevertheless, some (6) ...... (REVOLUTION) new designs, inspired by the (7) ...... (DISCOVER) that curved wings generated greater lift, led to the (8) ...... (APPEAR) of commercial airlines just a few decades later.
items.explanation: Adjective from REVOLUTION → **revolutionary** designs; noun from DISCOVER → the **discovery** about wings; noun from APPEAR → **appearance** of airlines.

items.sentence: One (9) ...... (RESEARCH) who has written extensively on the subject, Elena Marsh, has stated: 'The (10) ...... (IMPORTANT) of understanding this period for modern aerospace engineering cannot be overstated.'
items.explanation: Person who researches → **researcher**; noun from IMPORTANT → the **importance** of understanding this period.

exercises.title: B: Match to make sentences
exercises.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).
exercises.studentInstruction: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: My laptop has broken **D** down, so I'm afraid you'll have to wait until tomorrow.
items.answer: D – down, so I'm afraid you'll have to wait until tomorrow.
acceptedAnswers: D – down, so I'm afraid you'll have to wait until tomorrow.
items.explanation: Machine stopped working → laptop has broken **down**.

items.sentence: Several studies have been carried **A** out by physicists which prove that time can speed up and slow down.
items.answer: A – out by physicists which prove that time can speed up and slow down.
acceptedAnswers: A – out by physicists which prove that time can speed up and slow down.
items.explanation: Conduct research → studies have been carried **out** by physicists.

items.sentence: The engineer who came **F** up with that idea should have won the Nobel Prize.
items.answer: F – up with that idea should have won the Nobel Prize.
acceptedAnswers: F – up with that idea should have won the Nobel Prize.
items.explanation: Invent or devise → came **up with** that idea.

items.sentence: Our internet connection was cut **C** off for over three hours yesterday.
items.answer: C – off for over three hours yesterday.
acceptedAnswers: C – off for over three hours yesterday.
items.explanation: Connection lost → internet was cut **off** for three hours.

items.sentence: My history professor would love to find **H** out much more about technology in the Bronze Age.
items.answer: H – out much more about technology in the Bronze Age.
acceptedAnswers: H – out much more about technology in the Bronze Age.
items.explanation: Discover information → would love to find **out** more about Bronze Age technology.

items.sentence: That paint gives **E** off a very nasty smell when it's heated.
items.answer: E – off a very nasty smell when it's heated.
acceptedAnswers: E – off a very nasty smell when it's heated.
items.explanation: Emit → paint gives **off** a nasty smell when heated.

items.sentence: Mobile phone technology has come **B** on enormously in recent years.
items.answer: B – on enormously in recent years.
acceptedAnswers: B – on enormously in recent years.
items.explanation: Make progress → technology has come **on** enormously.

items.sentence: Could you possibly put me **G** through to the Radiology Department, please.
items.answer: G – through to the Radiology Department, please.
acceptedAnswers: G – through to the Radiology Department, please.
items.explanation: Connect a phone call → put me **through** to the Radiology Department.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
I can't wait until we do the outdoor fitness session on Saturday. **forward**
I'm really ...... the outdoor fitness session on Saturday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is eager anticipation, so the correct answer is "looking forward to doing".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "looking forward to doing", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Travel became much easier when budget airlines were introduced. **with**
Travel became much easier ...... budget airlines.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is when something was introduced, so the correct answer is "with the introduction of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "with the introduction of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
After months of rehearsals, the director was finally successful in bringing the play to the stage. **managed**
After months of rehearsals, the director ...... the play to the stage.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is succeeded after effort, so the correct answer is "finally managed to bring".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "finally managed to bring", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I can't understand how people manage to sleep on long-haul flights. **impossible**
I ...... how people manage to sleep on long-haul flights.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is cannot understand, so the correct answer is "find it impossible to understand".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "find it impossible to understand", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
A stroke of luck played a key role when Fleming discovered penicillin. **resulted**
A stroke of luck ...... penicillin.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is led to a discovery, so the correct answer is "resulted in Fleming's discovery of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "resulted in Fleming's discovery of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
People sometimes call firefighters 'everyday heroes'. **known**
Firefighters ...... 'everyday heroes'.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive + known as, so the correct answer is "are sometimes known as".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
The coach eventually realised that his tactics had been wrong all along. **conclusion**
The coach eventually ...... that his tactics had been wrong all along.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reached a decision, so the correct answer is "came to the conclusion".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "came to the conclusion", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The tour guide tried to describe the painting's historical significance. **attempt**
The tour guide ...... describe the painting's historical significance.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is tried to do something, so the correct answer is "made an attempt to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "made an attempt to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
We'll book the hotel and then we'll fly to Barcelona. **soon**
We'll fly to Barcelona ...... booked the hotel.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is one action immediately after another, so the correct answer is "as soon as we have".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "as soon as we have", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
